name: Social Proof
description: The psychological phenomenon where people assume the actions of others in an attempt to reflect correct behavior for a given situation. Highly effective for B2B SaaS and E-commerce.
how_to_apply: Integrate verified user testimonials, live activity counts ('120 people viewing'), trust badges (SOC2, GDPR), and review aggregates (stars) near primary CTAs.
anti_patterns: Fake or overly generic reviews (e.g., 'Great app! - John')
Aggressive FOMO popups ('Someone in Ohio just bought X') which degrade brand trust.
Stock photography for user avatars.
ui_examples: 'Trusted by' logo band in Hero section
Inline star ratings on pricing cards
Testimonial carousel

name: Scarcity & Urgency
description: Items appear more valuable when they are limited in availability (scarcity) or time (urgency). Triggers loss aversion.
how_to_apply: Display stock levels ('Only 2 left at this price'), limited-time countdowns for legitimate offers, or exclusive invite-only gates.
anti_patterns: Fake countdown timers that reset on page refresh.
False stock limits on digital goods without logical constraint.
Using red aggressively everywhere, causing sensory fatigue.
ui_examples: Inline stock indicator on product page
Countdown banner fixed to top of viewport
Expiring discount code on checkout modal

name: Loss Aversion
description: The pain of losing something is psychologically twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining the equivalent thing.
how_to_apply: Frame value propositions around what the user saves (time, money, effort) rather than just what they gain. Offer free trials that users don't want to give up.
anti_patterns: Dark patterns like 'Confirm shaming' (e.g., 'No thanks, I prefer paying full price').
Making cancellation intentionally difficult (Roach Motel).
ui_examples: 'You are saving $50 today' checkout summary
Trial expiration warning with 'Keep my data' CTA

name: Cognitive Ease (Hick's Law)
description: The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices. Simplify to reduce cognitive load.
how_to_apply: Group related actions, use progressive disclosure (show advanced options only when needed), and establish a clear visual hierarchy with one primary CTA.
anti_patterns: Presenting all features on a single screen without visual hierarchy.
Equal visual weight for Primary and Secondary buttons.
Jargon-heavy microcopy that requires mental translation.
ui_examples: Progressive disclosure accordion in settings
Multi-step wizard instead of a long single form
Clear Primary/Secondary/Tertiary button styling

name: Anchoring Effect
description: People rely heavily on the first piece of information offered (the 'anchor') when making decisions.
how_to_apply: Show the highest price tier first, or show the 'original price' crossed out next to the discounted price. Highlight a 'Recommended' tier to set the baseline expectation.
anti_patterns: Anchoring with an unrealistically high, fake price.
Failing to justify the value of the anchor tier.
ui_examples: Three-tier pricing table with 'Pro' highlighted
Strikethrough original price in cart

name: Zeigarnik Effect
description: People remember uncompleted or interrupted tasks better than completed tasks. Unfinished tasks create psychological tension.
how_to_apply: Use progress bars indicating a task is partially complete upon starting. Show 'profile completion' meters to drive data collection.
anti_patterns: Progress bars that move backward or stall unexpectedly.
Starting a progress bar at 0% (always start at >0% to trigger the Endowed Progress Effect).
ui_examples: Onboarding checklist widget
Profile completeness ring chart
Multi-step checkout breadcrumbs

name: Endowed Progress Effect
description: People are more motivated to complete a goal if they perceive they have already been given a head start.
how_to_apply: When a user signs up, give them the first onboarding step automatically 'checked off' (e.g., 'Account Created'). Pre-fill forms with known data.
anti_patterns: Making the head start feel trivial or fake.
Resetting progress without clear warning.
ui_examples: Loyalty card with first 2 stamps pre-filled
Checkout with 'Shipping Info' automatically pulled from Apple Pay

name: Reciprocity
description: When someone does something nice for you, you feel an urge to do something nice in return.
how_to_apply: Provide extreme upfront value (free tools, ungated templates, deep insights) before asking for an email or payment. The 'Give, Give, Give, Ask' framework.
anti_patterns: Gating basic functionality before demonstrating value.
Demanding contact info to read a basic blog post.
ui_examples: Interactive ROI calculator before email capture
7-day free trial without credit card required

name: Authority & Halo Effect
description: People tend to obey authority figures and transfer positive feelings about one trait (e.g., beautiful design) to the entire brand (Halo Effect).
how_to_apply: Use high-quality, premium UI to instantly communicate competence. Feature endorsements from industry experts or recognized publications.
anti_patterns: Appealing to irrelevant authorities.
Over-promising with authority figures while the actual product UX is broken.
ui_examples: 'Featured in TechCrunch' banner
Expert quote in hero section
Pixel-perfect, micro-animated interface signaling premium quality

name: Fitts's Law
description: The time required to rapidly move to a target area is a function of the ratio between the distance to the target and the width of the target.
how_to_apply: Make primary CTAs large and position them close to where the user's focus/cursor currently is. On mobile, keep primary actions in the bottom 'thumb zone'.
anti_patterns: Tiny hit areas for important actions.
Placing the 'Next' button far away from the last input field in a form.
ui_examples: Sticky bottom CTA bar on mobile
Large, full-width primary buttons in modals
Expanded padding around text links for larger tap target
